sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content: Surgical excision is the primary treatment for all stages of cutaneous melanoma and remains the only curative modality for localized disease. Surgery also plays important roles in staging (SLNB), regional control (CLND/TLND), and palliation of metastatic disease. Principles: 1. Wide excision margins are based on measured clinical margins taken at the time of surgery and defined by Breslow thickness [34]. 2. For melanoma in situ: excision with 0.5-1 cm margins. For large/poorly defined MIS or LM subtype, margins >0.5 cm may be necessary [34]. 3. For invasive melanoma ≤1.0 mm: 1 cm margin (category 1). For 1.01-2.0 mm: 1-2 cm margin (category 1). For >2.0 mm: 2 cm margin (category 1) [34]. 4. Surgical margins may be modified to accommodate individual anatomic or functional considerations, though narrower margins may increase risk of margin positivity [34]. 5. Depth of excision into subcutaneous fat may be adequate in anatomic locations where excision to fascia would cause significant morbidity [34]. 6. Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) is not recommended for primary treatment of invasive melanoma when standard clinical margins can be obtained. MMS may be considered selectively for minimally invasive (T1a) melanomas in anatomically constrained areas [34]. 7. Orient biopsy incision for definitive treatment (longitudinally on extremities, parallel to lymphatics) to not interfere with SLNB [29]. Resectability criteria: Disease is considered resectable when complete surgical removal with negative margins is technically feasible without unacceptable morbidity. Disease may be technically unresectable (involvement of major neurovascular structures) or clinically unresectable (distant nodal disease) where surgery alone would have minimal clinical benefit. Tumors that were locally advanced and unresectable that have become resectable should be considered for surgical resection [20], [40]. Pre-operative optimisation: For patients with borderline resectable or unresectable disease, neoadjuvant systemic therapy may convert borderline/unresectable disease to resectable, reduce surgical morbidity, allow targeted dissection of isolated nodal metastases, and potentially improve outcomes.

cards.name: Wide Local Excision
cards.detail: Approach: Surgical excision with margins based on Breslow thickness. Complete removal to level of fascia (typically preserved unless involved). Indication: Primary treatment for all invasive melanomas and definitive treatment for most melanoma in situ Key complications: Wound healing complications, scarring, functional/morbidity depending on anatomic location Operative mortality: Not reported as a specific metric; generally low for local procedures

cards.name: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (SLNB)
cards.detail: Approach: Preoperative lymphoscintigraphy with Tc-99m radiotracer injection, intraoperative identification using blue dye (isosulfan blue or methylene blue) and gamma probe. SLN(s) evaluated with serial sectioning and IHC [35], [36]. Indication: Clinical stage I/II melanoma. For T1b or T2a+, discussion and consideration of SLNB. Not recommended for stage IA (T1a <0.8 mm without ulceration) unless significant uncertainty about microstaging [5], [6], [36]. Key complications: Complication rate ~5-10%. Wound dehiscence/infection, seroma/hematoma, lymphedema (higher risk for groin vs axilla/neck), nerve injury, allergic reactions to blue dye [37], [38]. Operative mortality: Very low

cards.name: Completion Lymph Node Dissection (CLND)
cards.detail: Approach: Anatomically complete dissection of involved nodal basin after positive SLNB Indication: CLND has no proven impact on MSS or OS based on MSLT-II and DeCOG trials. Active nodal basin US surveillance preferred [18], [19]. May be considered in select cases where adherence to surveillance is uncertain or high likelihood of additional positive nodes. Key complications: Wound dehiscence/infection, hematoma/seroma, neuropathy, lymphocele, lymphedema (20-50%), wound complications [38], [39]. Operative mortality: Low

cards.name: Therapeutic Lymph Node Dissection (TLND)
cards.detail: Approach: Dissection of involved nodal basin for clinically palpable nodal disease. Head/neck: superficial parotidectomy + neck dissection. Axilla: levels I-III. Groin: inguinofemoral dissection; pelvic LND considered for clinically positive inguinofemoral nodes or Cloquet's node positive [20], [39]. Indication: Clinically positive regional lymph nodes without distant metastases Key complications: Same as CLND; 5-year survival 30-50% depending on number of nodes involved, extracapsular extension, and primary tumor features [39]. Operative mortality: Low

cards.name: Metastasectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Resection of oligometastatic disease Indication: Selected patients with limited metastatic disease amenable to complete resection Key complications: Procedure-specific morbidity Operative mortality: Variable by site and extent
